reason: Genuine Python antipattern: mutable default argument `defaults={}` is shared across all calls and could lead to silent state bleed if the function is extended to mutate it; the anchor resolves correctly to line 4 RIGHT in the diff.
comment: Mutable default argument: `defaults={}` creates a single dict object that is shared across all calls. If any caller or future code mutates the returned `data` dict in a way that bleeds back, or if the function body is extended to write to `defaults`, the mutation persists silently between calls. Use `defaults=None` and replace with `if defaults is None: defaults = {}` at the top of the function body.
